title: Hornbostel–Sachs

infobox.value: or **Sachs–Hornbostel** is a system of [musical instrument classification](/wiki/Musical_instrument_classification "Musical instrument classification") devised by [Erich Moritz von Hornbostel](/wiki/Erich_Moritz_von_Hornbostel "Erich Moritz von Hornbostel") and [Curt Sachs](/wiki/Curt_Sachs "Curt Sachs"). This system was first published in the *Zeitschrift für Ethnologie* in 1914.[[1]](#cite_note-1) An English translation was published in the *[Galpin Society Journal](/wiki/Galpin_Society "Galpin Society")* in 1961, which is the most widely used system for classifying [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instruments "Musical instruments") by [ethnomusicologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology") and [organologists](/wiki/Organology "Organology") (people who study musical instruments). The system was updated in 2011 as part of the work of the Musical Instrument Museums Online (MIMO) Project.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

primarily produce their sounds by means of the actual body of the instrument vibrating, rather than a string, membrane, or column of air. In essence, this group includes all [percussion instruments](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") apart from [drums](/wiki/Drums "Drums"), and some other instruments. In the Hornbostel–Sachs classification, idiophones are first categorized according to the method used to play the instrument. The result is four main categories: struck idiophones (11), plucked idiophones (12), friction idiophones (13) and blown idiophones (14). These groups are subsequently divided through various criteria. In many cases these sub-categories are split in singular specimens and sets of instruments. The class of idiophones includes the [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), the [marimba](/wiki/Marimba "Marimba"), the [glockenspiel](/wiki/Glockenspiel "Glockenspiel"), and the [glass harmonica](/wiki/Glass_harmonica "Glass harmonica").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Musical instrument classification system

**Hornbostel–Sachs** or **Sachs–Hornbostel** is a system of [musical instrument classification](/wiki/Musical_instrument_classification "Musical instrument classification") devised by [Erich Moritz von Hornbostel](/wiki/Erich_Moritz_von_Hornbostel "Erich Moritz von Hornbostel") and [Curt Sachs](/wiki/Curt_Sachs "Curt Sachs"). This system was first published in the *Zeitschrift für Ethnologie* in 1914.[[1]](#cite_note-1) An English translation was published in the *[Galpin Society Journal](/wiki/Galpin_Society "Galpin Society")* in 1961, which is the most widely used system for classifying [musical instruments](/wiki/Musical_instruments "Musical instruments") by [ethnomusicologists](/wiki/Ethnomusicology "Ethnomusicology") and [organologists](/wiki/Organology "Organology") (people who study musical instruments). The system was updated in 2011 as part of the work of the Musical Instrument Museums Online (MIMO) Project.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Hornbostel and Sachs based their ideas on a system devised in the late 19th century by [Victor-Charles Mahillon](/wiki/Victor-Charles_Mahillon "Victor-Charles Mahillon"), the curator of musical instruments at [Brussels Conservatory](/wiki/Brussels_Conservatory "Brussels Conservatory"). Mahillon divided instruments into four broad categories according to the nature of the sound-producing material: an air column; string; membrane; and body of the instrument. From this basis, Hornbostel and Sachs expanded Mahillon's system to make it possible to classify any instrument from any culture.

Formally, the Hornbostel–Sachs is modeled on the [Dewey Decimal Classification](/wiki/Dewey_Decimal_Classification "Dewey Decimal Classification") for libraries. It has five top-level classifications, with several levels below those, adding up to over 300 basic categories in total.

## Idiophones (1)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Idiophones (1)")]

Main article: [Idiophone](/wiki/Idiophone "Idiophone")

See also: [List of idiophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number](/wiki/List_of_idiophones_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number "List of idiophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number")

**[Idiophones](/wiki/Idiophone "Idiophone")** primarily produce their sounds by means of the actual body of the instrument vibrating, rather than a string, membrane, or column of air. In essence, this group includes all [percussion instruments](/wiki/Percussion_instrument "Percussion instrument") apart from [drums](/wiki/Drums "Drums"), and some other instruments. In the Hornbostel–Sachs classification, idiophones are first categorized according to the method used to play the instrument. The result is four main categories: struck idiophones (11), plucked idiophones (12), friction idiophones (13) and blown idiophones (14). These groups are subsequently divided through various criteria. In many cases these sub-categories are split in singular specimens and sets of instruments. The class of idiophones includes the [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), the [marimba](/wiki/Marimba "Marimba"), the [glockenspiel](/wiki/Glockenspiel "Glockenspiel"), and the [glass harmonica](/wiki/Glass_harmonica "Glass harmonica").

### Struck idiophones (11)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Struck idiophones (11)")]

Main article: [Struck idiophone](/wiki/Struck_idiophone "Struck idiophone")

These idiophones are set in vibration by being struck, for example [cymbals](/wiki/Cymbal "Cymbal") or [xylophones](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone").

#### Directly struck idiophones (111)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Directly struck idiophones (111)")]

The player executes the movement of striking; whether by mechanical intermediate devices, beaters, keyboards, or by pulling ropes, etc. It is definitive that the player can apply clear, exact, individual strokes, and that the instrument itself is equipped for this kind of percussion.

* 111.1 [Concussion idiophones](/wiki/Concussion_idiophone "Concussion idiophone") or [clappers](/wiki/Clapper_(musical_instrument) "Clapper (musical instrument)") – Two or more complementary sonorous parts are struck against each other.
  + 111.11 Concussion [sticks](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/stick "wikt:stick") or stick clappers ([clapstick](/wiki/Clapstick "Clapstick")).
  + 111.12 Concussion plaques or plaque clappers ([paiban](/wiki/Paiban "Paiban")).
  + 111.13 Concussion [troughs](/wiki/Trough_(physics) "Trough (physics)") or [trough](/wiki/Watering_trough "Watering trough") clappers ([devil chase](/wiki/Devil_chase "Devil chase")).
  + 111.14 Concussion [vessels](/wiki/Bowl_(vessel) "Bowl (vessel)") or vessel clappers ([spoons](/wiki/Spoon_(musical_instrument) "Spoon (musical instrument)")).
    - 111.141 [Castanets](/wiki/Castanets "Castanets") – Natural and hollowed-out vessel clappers.
    - 111.142 [Cymbals](/wiki/Cymbals "Cymbals") – Vessel clappers with manufactured rim.
* 111.2 [Percussion idiophones](/wiki/Percussion_idiophone "Percussion idiophone") – The instrument is struck either with a non-sonorous object (hand, stick, striker) or against a non-sonorous object (human body, the ground).
  + 111.21 [Percussion sticks](/wiki/Percussion_stick "Percussion stick").
    - 111.211 Individual percussion sticks.
    - 111.212 Sets of percussion sticks in a range of different pitches combined into one instrument. – All [xylophones](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), as long as their sounding components are not in two different planes.
  + 111.22 [Percussion plaques](/wiki/Percussion_plaque "Percussion plaque").
    - 111.221 Individual percussion plaques.
    - 111.222 Sets of percussion plaques – Examples are the [lithophone](/wiki/Lithophone "Lithophone") and also most [metallophones](/wiki/Metallophone "Metallophone").
  + 111.23 [Percussion tubes](/wiki/Percussion_tube "Percussion tube").
    - 111.231 Individual percussion tubes.
    - 111.232 Sets of percussion tubes.
  + 111.24 [Percussion vessels](/wiki/Percussion_vessel "Percussion vessel").
    - 111.241 [Gongs](/wiki/Gong "Gong") – The vibration is strongest near the vertex.
      * 111.241.1 Individual gongs.
      * 111.241.2 Sets of gongs.
    - 111.242 [Bells](/wiki/Bell "Bell") – The vibration is weakest near the vertex.
      * 111.242.1 Individual bells.
        + 111.242.11 Resting bells whose opening faces upward (for example the [standing bell](/wiki/Standing_bell "Standing bell"))
        + 111.242.12 Hanging bells suspended from the apex.
          - 111.242.121 Hanging bells without internal strikers.
          - 111.242.122 Hanging bells with internal strikers.
      * 111.242.2 Sets of bells or [chimes](/wiki/Tubular_bells "Tubular bells").
        + 111.242.21 Sets of resting bells whose opening faces upward.
        + 111.242.22 Sets of hanging bells suspended from the apex.
          - 111.242.221 Sets of hanging bells without internal strikers.
          - 111.242.222 Sets of hanging bells with internal strikers.
  + 111.3 Mixed sets of directly struck idiophones

#### Indirectly struck idiophones (112)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Indirectly struck idiophones (112)")]

The player themself does not go through the movement of striking; percussion results indirectly through some other movement by the player.

* 112.1 Shaken Idiophones or [rattles](/wiki/Rattle_(percussion_instrument) "Rattle (percussion instrument)") – The player makes a shaking motion
  + 112.11 Suspension rattles – Perforated idiophones are mounted together, and shaken to strike against each other.
    - 112.111 Strung rattles – Rattling objects are strung in rows on a cord.
    - 112.112 Stick rattles – Rattling objects are strung on a bar or ring.
  + 112.12 Frame rattles – Rattling objects are attached to a carrier against which they strike ([flexatone](/wiki/Flexatone "Flexatone")).
    - 112.121 Pendant rattles.
    - 112.122 Sliding rattles.
  + 112.13 Vessel rattles – Rattling objects enclosed in a vessel strike against each other or against the walls of the vessel, or usually against both.
* 112.2 [Scraped Idiophones](/wiki/Scraped_idiophone "Scraped idiophone") – The player causes a scraping movement directly or indirectly; a non-sonorous object moves along the notched surface of a sonorous object, to be alternately lifted off the teeth and flicked against them; or an elastic sonorous object moves along the surface of a notched non-sonorous object to cause a series of impacts. This group must not be confused with that of friction idiophones.
  + 112.21 Scraped sticks.
    - 112.211 Scraped sticks without resonator.
    - 112.212 Scraped sticks with resonator.
  + 112.22 Scraped tubes.
    - 112.221 Scraped tubes without resonator.
    - 112.222 Scraped tubes with resonator.
  + 112.23 Scraped vessels.
    - 112.231 Scraped vessels without resonator.
    - 112.232 Scraped vessels with resonator.
  + 112.24 Scraped wheels – cog rattles or [Ratchet](/wiki/Ratchet_(instrument) "Ratchet (instrument)")
    - 112.241 Scraped wheels without resonator.
    - 112.242 Scraped wheels with resonator.
* 112.3 Split idiophones – Instruments in the shape of two springy arms connected at one end and touching at the other: the arms are forced apart by a little stick, to jangle or vibrate on recoil.

### Plucked idiophones (12)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Plucked idiophones (12)")]

Further information: [List of lamellophones](/wiki/List_of_lamellophones "List of lamellophones")

Plucked idiophones, or [lamellaphones](/wiki/Lamellaphone "Lamellaphone"), are idiophones set in vibration by being plucked; examples include the [jaw harp](/wiki/Jaw_harp "Jaw harp") or [mbira](/wiki/Mbira "Mbira"). This group is sub-divided in the following two categories:

#### In the form of a frame (121)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In the form of a frame (121)")]

The lamellae vibrate within a frame or hoop.

* 121.1 Clack idiophones or [Cricri](/wiki/Cricri "Cricri") – The lamella is carved in the surface of a fruit shell, which serves as resonator.
* 121.2 Guimbardes and [Jaw harps](/wiki/Jaw_harp "Jaw harp") (i. e. "kissed" idiophones) – The lamella is mounted in a rod- or plaque-shaped frame and depends on the player's mouth cavity for resonance.
  + 121.21 Idioglot guimbardes – The lamella is cut through the frame of the instrument ([kubing](/wiki/Kubing "Kubing")).
    - 121.211 Individual idioglot guimbardes.
    - 121.212 Sets of idioglot guimbardes
  + 121.22 Heteroglot guimbardes – The lamella is attached to the frame (Western Jew's harp, [kouxian](/wiki/Kouxian "Kouxian")). Present-day ethnomusicologists, such as [Margaret Kartomi](/wiki/Margaret_J._Kartomi "Margaret J. Kartomi") (page 173) and Ellingson (PhD dissertation, 1979, p. 544), might support the suggestion that, in keeping with the spirit of the original Hornbostel–Sachs classification scheme, of categorization by what first produces the initial sound in the instrument, that the supposed class 412.13 should count as these instead.
    - 121.221 Individual heteroglot guimbardes.
    - 121.222 Sets of heteroglot guimbardes
  + 121.23 Mixed sets of guimbardes

#### In the form of a comb (122)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In the form of a comb (122)")]

The lamellae are tied to a board or cut out from a board like the teeth of a comb.

* 122.1 With laced on lamellae.
  + 122.11 Without resonator.
  + 122.12 With resonator.
* 122.2 With cut-out lamellae – [Musical box](/wiki/Musical_box "Musical box")
  + 122.21 Without resonator.
  + 122.22 With resonator.
* 122.3 Mixed sets of combs

#### Mixed sets of lamellophones (123)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Mixed sets of lamellophones (123)")]

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Friction idiophones (13)")]

Idiophones which are rubbed, for example the [nail violin](/wiki/Nail_violin "Nail violin"), a bowed instrument with solid pieces of metal or wood rather than strings.

  + 131.11 Without direct friction.
  + 131.12 With direct friction.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Friction plaques (132)")]

* 132.1 Individual friction plaques.
  + 132.11 Without direct friction.
  + 132.12 With direct friction.
* 132.2 Sets of friction plaques.
  + 132.21 Without direct friction.
  + 132.22 With direct friction.
  + 132.23 Mixed

#### Friction vessels (133)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Friction vessels (133)")]

* 133.1 Individual friction vessels (for example the [singing bowl](/wiki/Singing_bowl "Singing bowl"))
  + 133.11 Without direct friction.

### Blown idiophones (14)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Blown idiophones (14)")]

Main article: [Blown idiophone](/wiki/Blown_idiophone "Blown idiophone")

Blown idiophones are those set in vibration by the movement of air, for example the [Aeolsklavier](/wiki/Aeolsklavier "Aeolsklavier"), an instrument consisting of several pieces of wood which vibrate when air is blown onto them by a set of [bellows](/wiki/Bellows "Bellows"). The piano chanteur features plaques.

## Membranophones (2)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Membranophones (2)")]

Main article: [Membranophone](/wiki/Membranophone "Membranophone")

See also: [List of membranophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number](/wiki/List_of_membranophones_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number "List of membranophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number")

**[Membranophones](/wiki/Membranophone "Membranophone")** primarily produce their sounds by means of the vibration of a tightly stretched membrane. This group includes all [drums](/wiki/Drum "Drum") and [kazoos](/wiki/Kazoo "Kazoo").

### Struck membranophones (21)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Struck membranophones (21)")]

Struck drums are instruments that have a struck membrane. This includes most types of drums, such as the [timpani](/wiki/Timpani "Timpani"), or [kettle drums](/wiki/Kettle_drums "Kettle drums"), and the [snare drum](/wiki/Snare_drum "Snare drum").

#### Directly struck membranophones (211)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Directly struck membranophones (211)")]

Main article: [Directly struck membranophone](/wiki/Directly_struck_membranophone "Directly struck membranophone")

Instruments in which the membrane is struck directly, such as through bare hands, beaters or keyboards.

* 211.1 Instruments in which the body of the drum is dish- or bowl-shaped ([kettle drums](/wiki/Kettle_drum "Kettle drum"))
  + 211.11 Single instruments
  + 211.12 Sets of instruments
* 211.2 Instruments in which the body is tubular ([tubular drums](/wiki/Tubular_drum "Tubular drum"))
  + 211.21 Instruments in which the body has the same diameter at the middle and end ([cylindrical drums](/wiki/Cylindrical_drum "Cylindrical drum"))
    - 211.211 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
      * 211.211.1 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
      * 211.211.2 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
    - 211.212 Instruments which have two usable membranes
      * 211.212.1 Single instruments
      * 211.212.2 Sets of instruments
    - 211.213 Mixed sets of cylindrical drums
  + 211.22 Instruments in which the body is barrel-shaped ([barrel drums](/wiki/Barrel_drum "Barrel drum"))
    - 211.221 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
      * 211.221.1 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
      * 211.221.2 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
    - 211.222 Instruments which have two usable membranes
      * 211.222.1 Single instruments
      * 211.222.2 Sets of instruments
    - 211.223 Mixed sets of barrel drums
  + 211.23 Instruments in which the body is hourglass-shaped
    - 211.231 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
      * 211.231.1 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
      * 211.231.2 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
    - 211.232 Instruments which have two usable membranes
      * 211.232.1 Single instruments
      * 211.232.2 Sets of instruments
    - 211.233 Mixed sets of hourglass drums
  + 211.24 Instruments in which the body is conical-shaped ([conical drums](/wiki/Conical_drum "Conical drum"))
    - 211.241 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
      * 211.241.1 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
      * 211.241.2 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
    - 211.242 Instruments which have two usable membranes
      * 211.242.1 Single instruments
      * 211.242.2 Sets of instruments
    - 211.243 Mixed sets of single-conical drums
    - 211.244 Instruments in which the body is double-conical
      * 211.244.1 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
        + 211.244.11 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
        + 211.244.12 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
      * 211.244.2 Instruments which have two usable membranes
        + 211.244.21 Single instruments
        + 211.244.22 Sets of instruments
    - 211.245 Mixed sets of double-conical drums
    - 211.246 Mixed sets of conical drums
  + 211.25 Instruments in which the body is goblet-shaped ([goblet drums](/wiki/Goblet_drum "Goblet drum"))
    - 211.251 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
      * 211.251.1 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is open
      * 211.251.2 Instruments in which the end without a membrane is closed
    - 211.252 Instruments which have two usable membranes
      * 211.252.1 Single instruments
      * 211.252.2 Sets of instruments
    - 211.253 Mixed sets of goblet drums
  + 211.26 Mixed sets of tubular drums
* 211.3 Instruments in which the body depth is not greater than the radius of the membrane ([frame drums](/wiki/Frame_drum "Frame drum")).
  + 211.31 Instruments which do not have a handle
    - 211.311 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
    - 211.312 Instruments which have two usable membranes
  + 211.32 Instruments which have a handle
    - 211.321 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
    - 211.322 Instruments which have two usable membranes

#### Shaken membranophones (212)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Shaken membranophones (212)")]

Instruments that are shaken, the membrane being vibrated by objects inside the drum ([rattle drums](/wiki/Rattle_drum "Rattle drum")).

### Plucked membranophones (22)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Plucked membranophones (22)")]

Instruments with a string attached to the membrane, so that when the string is plucked, the membrane vibrates (plucked drums).  
Some commentators believe that instruments in this class ought instead to be regarded as chordophones (see below).

### Friction membranophones (23)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Friction membranophones (23)")]

Instruments in which the membrane vibrates as a result of friction. These are drums which are rubbed, rather than being struck.

#### Friction drums with stick (231)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Friction drums with stick (231)")]

Instruments in which the membrane is vibrated from a stick that is rubbed or used to rub the membrane

* 231.1 Instruments in which the stick is inserted in a hole in the membrane
  + 231.11 Instruments in which the stick can not be moved and is subject to rubbing, causing friction on the membrane
  + 231.12 Instruments in which the stick is semi-movable, and can be used to rub the membrane
  + 231.13 Instruments in which the stick is freely movable, and is used to rub the membrane
* 231.2 Instruments in which the stick is tied upright to the membrane

#### Friction drum with cord (232)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Friction drum with cord (232)")]

Instruments in which a cord, attached to the membrane, is rubbed.

* 232.1 Instruments in which the drum is held stationary while playing
  + 232.11 Instruments which have only one usable membrane
  + 232.12 Instruments which have two usable membranes
* 232.2 Instruments in which the drum is twirled by a cord, which rubs in a notch on the stick held by the player

#### Hand friction drums (233)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Hand friction drums (233)")]

Instruments in which the membrane is rubbed by hand

### Singing membranes (kazoos) (24)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Singing membranes (kazoos) (24)")]

Main article: [Kazoo](/wiki/Kazoo "Kazoo")

This group includes [kazoos](/wiki/Kazoo "Kazoo"), instruments which do not produce sound of their own, but modify other sounds by way of a vibrating membrane.

#### Free kazoos (241)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Free kazoos (241)")]

Instruments in which the membrane is vibrated by an unbroken column of wind, without a chamber

#### Tube or vessel-kazoos (242)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Tube or vessel-kazoos (242)")]

Instruments in which the membrane is placed in a box, tube or other container

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Unclassified membranophones (25)")]

## Chordophones (3)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Chordophones (3)")]

Main article: [Chordophone](/wiki/Chordophone "Chordophone")

See also: [List of chordophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number](/wiki/List_of_chordophones_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number "List of chordophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number")

**[Chordophones](/wiki/Chordophone "Chordophone")** primarily produce their sounds by means of the vibration of a string or strings that are stretched between fixed points. This group includes all instruments generally called [string instruments](/wiki/String_instrument "String instrument") in the west, as well as many (but not all) [keyboard instruments](/wiki/Keyboard_instrument "Keyboard instrument"), such as [pianos](/wiki/Piano "Piano") and [harpsichords](/wiki/Harpsichord "Harpsichord").

### Simple chordophones or [zithers](/wiki/Zither "Zither") (31)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Simple chordophones or zithers (31)")]

Instruments which are in essence simply a string or strings and a string bearer. These instruments may have a resonator box, but removing it should not render the instrument unplayable, though it may result in quite a different sound being produced. They include the [piano](/wiki/Piano "Piano") therefore, as well as other kinds of [zithers](/wiki/Zither "Zither") such as the [koto](/wiki/Koto_(musical_instrument) "Koto (musical instrument)"), and [musical bows](/wiki/Musical_bow "Musical bow").

#### [Bar zithers](/wiki/Bar_zither "Bar zither") (311)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Bar zithers (311)")]

Main article: [Bar zither](/wiki/Bar_zither "Bar zither")

The string bearer is bar-shaped.

* 311.1 [Musical bows](/wiki/Musical_bow "Musical bow") – The string bearer is flexible (and curved)
  + 311.11 Idiochord musical bows – The string is cut from the bark of the cane, remaining attached at each end
    - 311.111 Mono-idiochord musical bows – Containing one string only
    - 311.112 Poly-idiochord musical bows or harp-bows – Containing several strings that pass over some type of bridge
  + 311.12 Heterochord musical bows – The string is of separate material from the bearer
    - 311.121 Mono-heterochord musical bows – The bow has one heterochord string only
      * 311.121.1 Without resonator
        + 311.121.11 Without tuning noose
        + 311.121.12 With tuning noose
      * 311.121.2 With resonator
        + 311.121.21 With independent resonator
          - 311.121.211 Without tuning noose
          - 311.121.212 With tuning noose
        + 311.121.22 With resonator attached
          - 311.121.221 Without tuning noose
          - 311.121.222 With tuning noose
    - 311.122 Poly-heterochord musical bows – The bow has several heterochord strings
      * 311.122.1 Without tuning noose
      * 311.122.2 With tuning noose
* 311.2 [Stick zithers](/wiki/Stick_zither "Stick zither") – With rigid string carrier
  + 311.21 Musical bow/stick – The string carrier has one rigid and one flexible end
    - [311.211](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_311.211 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 311.211") Instrument has one resonator gourd
    - [311.212](/w/index.php?title=List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_311.212&action=edit&redlink=1 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 311.212 (page does not exist)") Instrument has several resonator gourds
  + 311.22 True stick zithers – NB Round sticks which happen to be hollow by chance do not belong on this account to the tube zithers, but are round-bar zithers; however, instruments in which a tubular cavity is employed as a true resonator, like the modern Mexican harpa, are tube zithers
    - [311.221](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel-Sachs_number:_311.221 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel-Sachs number: 311.221") With one resonator gourd
    - [311.222](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel-Sachs_number:_311.222 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel-Sachs number: 311.222") With several resonator gourds

#### Tube zithers (312)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Tube zithers (312)")]

Main article: [Tube zither](/wiki/Tube_zither "Tube zither")

  + 312.11 Idiochord tube zithers

  + 312.21 Idiochord half-tube zithers

  + [312.22](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_312.22 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 312.22") Heterochord half-tube zithers
    - 312.221 Without extra resonator
    - 312.222 With extra resonator

#### Raft zithers (313)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Raft zithers (313)")]

### Composite chordophones (32)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Composite chordophones (32)")]

Acoustic and electro-acoustic instruments which have a resonator as an integral part of the instrument, and solid-body electric chordophones. This includes most western string instruments, including [lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute")-type instruments such as [violins](/wiki/Violin "Violin") and [guitars](/wiki/Guitar "Guitar"), and [harps](/wiki/Harp "Harp").

#### Lutes (321)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Lutes (321)")]

Main article: [Lute](/wiki/Lute "Lute")

The plane of the strings runs parallel with the resonator's surface.

* 321.1 Bow lutes – Each string has its own flexible carrier
* 321.2 Yoke lutes or lyres – The strings are attached to a yoke which lies in the same plane as the sound-table and consists of two arms and a cross-bar

* 321.3 Handle lutes – The string bearer is a plain handle
  + [321.31](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_321.31 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 321.31") Spike lutes

    - [321.314](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_321.314 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 321.314") Spike frame lutes
  + 321.32 Necked lutes
    - [321.321](/wiki/List_of_musical_instruments_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number:_321.321 "List of musical instruments by Hornbostel–Sachs number: 321.321") Necked bowl lute – [Mandolin](/wiki/Mandolin "Mandolin"), [Balalaika](/wiki/Balalaika "Balalaika"), etc.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Harps (322)")]

The plane of the strings lies perpendicular to the resonator's surface.

* 322.1 Open harps – The harp has no pillar
  + 322.11 [Arched harps](/wiki/Arched_harp "Arched harp")
  + 322.12 [Angular harps](/wiki/Angular_harp "Angular harp")

      * 322.222.2 With strings in two planes crossing each other

#### Harp lutes (323)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Harp lutes (323)")]

The plane of the strings lies at right angles to the sound-table; a line joining the lower ends of the strings would be perpendicular to the neck. These have notched bridges.

## Aerophones (4)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Aerophones (4)")]

Main article: [Aerophone](/wiki/Aerophone "Aerophone")

See also: [List of aerophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number](/wiki/List_of_aerophones_by_Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs_number "List of aerophones by Hornbostel–Sachs number")

Aerophones primarily produce their sounds by means of vibrating air. The instrument itself does not vibrate, and there are no vibrating strings or membranes.

### Free aerophones (41)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Free aerophones (41)")]

Instruments in which the vibrating air is not contained within the instrument, for example, [acme sirens](/wiki/Acme_siren "Acme siren") or the [bullroarer](/wiki/Bullroarer_(music) "Bullroarer (music)").

#### Displacement free aerophones (411)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Displacement free aerophones (411)")]

The air-stream meets a sharp edge, or a sharp edge is moved through the air. In either case, according to more recent views, a periodic displacement of air occurs to the alternate flanks of the edge. Examples are the swordblade or the whip.

#### Interruptive free aerophones (412)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Interruptive free aerophones (412)")]

The air-stream is interrupted periodically.

* 412.1 Idiophonic interruptive aerophones or reeds – The air-stream is directed against a lamella, setting it in periodic vibration to interrupt the stream intermittently. In this group also belong reeds with a 'cover,' i.e. a tube in which the air vibrates only in a secondary sense, not producing the sound but simply adding roundness and timbre to the sound made by the reed's vibration; generally recognizable by the absence of fingerholes though present-day ethnomusicologists, such as Margaret Kartomi (page 173) and Ellingson (PhD dissertation, 1979, p. 544), might support the suggestion that, in keeping with the spirit of the original Hornbostel–Sachs classification scheme, of categorization by what first produces the initial sound in the instrument, that such reeds should not really remain as aerophones, exiting for the lamellophones.
  + 412.11 Concussion reeds – Two lamellae make a gap which closes periodically during their vibration.
    - 412.111 Independent pairs of concussion reeds.
    - 412.112 Paired sets of concussion reeds
  + 412.12 Percussion reeds – A single lamella strikes against a frame.
    - 412.121 Independent percussion reeds.
    - 412.122 Sets of percussion reeds. – Earlier organs
  + 412.13 [Free-reed instruments](/wiki/Free-reed_instrument "Free-reed instrument") feature a reed which vibrates within a closely fitting slot (there may be an attached pipe, but it should only vibrate in sympathy with the reed, and not have an effect on the pitch – instruments of this class can be distinguished from 422.3 by the lack of finger-holes).
    - 412.131 Individual free reeds.
    - 412.132 Sets of free reeds – [Accordion](/wiki/Accordion "Accordion"), [harmonica](/wiki/Harmonica "Harmonica"), and [reed pipes](/wiki/Reed_pipe "Reed pipe") of the [pipe organ](/wiki/Pipe_organ "Pipe organ").
  + 412.14 Band reed instruments – The air hits the sharp edge of a band under tension. The acoustics of this instrument have so far not been investigated.[[3]](#cite_note-3)
    - 412.141 Individual band reeds.
    - 412.142 Sets of band reeds
  + 412.15 Mixed sets of reeds
* 412.2 Non-idiophonic interruptive instruments. The interruptive agent is not a reed.
  + 412.21 Rotating aerophones (interruptive agent rotates in its own plane and does not turn on its axis)- [Siren disk](/wiki/Siren_disk "Siren disk").
    - 412.211 Independent disks.
    - 412.212 Sets of disks
  + 412.22 Whirling aerophones (interruptive agent turns on its axis) – [Bullroarer](/wiki/Bullroarer "Bullroarer"), [whirly tube](/wiki/Whirly_tube "Whirly tube").
    - 412.221 Independent tubes.
    - 412.222 Sets of tubes
  + 412.23 Sets of disks and tubes
* 412.3 Mixed sets of interruptive aerophones

#### Plosive aerophones (413)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Plosive aerophones (413)")]

The sound is caused by a single compression and release of air. Examples include the [botija](/wiki/Botija_(instrument) "Botija (instrument)"), the [gharha](/wiki/Gharha "Gharha"), the [ghatam](/wiki/Ghatam "Ghatam"), and the [udu](/wiki/Udu "Udu").

**Mixed sets of free aerophones (414)**

### Non-free aerophones (wind instruments proper) (42)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=48 "Edit section: Non-free aerophones (wind instruments proper) (42)")]

The vibrating air is contained within the instrument. This group includes most of the instruments called [wind instruments](/wiki/Wind_instrument "Wind instrument") in the west, such as the [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute") or [French horn](/wiki/French_horn "French horn"), as well as many other kinds of instruments such as [conch shells](/wiki/Conch_shell "Conch shell").

  + 421.11 End-blown flutes – The player blows against the sharp rim at the upper open end of a tube.

        + 421.112.11 Open (raft) panpipes – The pipes are tied together in the form of a board, or they are made by drilling tubes \*in a board.
        + 421.112.12 Open bundle (pan-) pipes – The pipes are tied together in a round bundle.
      * 421.112.2 Stopped panpipes.
        + 421.112.21 Stopped (raft) panpipes – The pipes are tied together in the form of a board, or they are made by drilling tubes \*in a board.

        + 421.121.12 With fingerholes – [Western concert flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute").
      * 421.121.2 Partly stopped side-blown flutes – The lower end of the tube is a natural node of the pipe pierced by a small hole.
        + 421.121.21 Without fingerholes.
        + 421.121.22 With fingerholes
      * 421.121.3 Stopped side-blown flutes.
        + 421.121.31 Without fingerholes.

  + 421.21 Flutes with external duct – The duct is outside the wall of the flute; this group includes flutes with the duct chamfered in the wall under a ring-like sleeve and other similar arrangements.

## Electrophones (5)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=54 "Edit section: Electrophones (5)")]

Main article: [Electrophone](/wiki/Electrophone "Electrophone")

* 51. Instruments having electric action (e.g. pipe organ with electrically controlled solenoid air valves);
* 52. Instruments having electrical amplification, such as the [Neo-Bechstein](/wiki/Neo-Bechstein "Neo-Bechstein") piano of 1931, which had 18 microphones built into it;
* 53. *Radioelectric instruments*: instruments in which sound is produced by electrical means.

The fifth top-level group, the **[electrophones](/wiki/Electrophone "Electrophone")** category, was added by Sachs in 1940, to describe instruments involving electricity. Sachs broke down his 5th category into 3 subcategories: 51=electrically actuated acoustic instruments; 52=electrically amplified acoustic instruments; 53= instruments which make sound primarily by way of electrically driven oscillators, such as [theremins](/wiki/Theremin "Theremin") or [synthesizers](/wiki/Synthesizer "Synthesizer"), which he called radioelectric instruments. [Francis William Galpin](/wiki/Francis_William_Galpin "Francis William Galpin") provided such a group in his own classification system, which is closer to Mahillon than Sachs–Hornbostel. For example, in Galpin's 1937 book *A Textbook of European Musical Instruments*, he lists electrophones with three second-level divisions for sound generation ("by oscillation", "electro-magnetic", and "electro-static"), as well as third-level and fourth-level categories based on the control method. Sachs himself proposed subcategories 51, 52, and 53, on pages 447–467 of his 1940 book *The History of Musical Instruments*.

Present-day ethnomusicologists, such as Margaret Kartomi[[4]](#cite_note-4) and Ellingson (PhD dissertation, 1979, p. 544), suggest that, in keeping with the spirit of the original Hornbostel–Sachs classification scheme, of categorization by what first produces the initial sound in the instrument, that only subcategory 53 should remain in the electrophones category. Thus it has been more recently proposed that, for example, the pipe organ (even if it uses electric key action to control solenoid valves) remain in the aerophones category, and that the electric guitar remain in the chordophones category, etc.

## Application of the system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=55 "Edit section: Application of the system")]

Beyond the top three groups are several further levels of classification, so that the [xylophone](/wiki/Xylophone "Xylophone"), for example, is in the group labeled 111.212 (periods are usually added after every third digit to make long numbers easier to read). A long classification number does not necessarily indicate the instrument is a complicated one. The [valveless bugle](/wiki/Bugle_(instrument) "Bugle (instrument)"), for instance, has the classification number 423.121.22, even though it is generally regarded as a relatively simple instrument. The numbers in the bugle's classification indicate the following:

* **4** – an aerophone
* **42** – the vibrating air is enclosed within the instrument
* **423** – the player's lips cause the air to vibrate directly (as opposed to an instrument with a reed like a [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet"), or an edge-blown instrument, like a [flute](/wiki/Flute "Flute"))
* **423.1** – the player's lips are the only means of changing the instrument's pitch (that is, there are no valves as on a trumpet)
* **423.12** – the instrument is tubular, rather than being a [conch](/wiki/Conch "Conch")-type instrument
* **423.121** – the player blows into the end of the tube, as opposed to the side of the tube
* **423.121.2** – the tube is bent or folded, as opposed to straight
* **423.121.22** – the instrument has a [mouthpiece](/wiki/Mouthpiece_(brass) "Mouthpiece (brass)")

423.121.22 does not uniquely identify the bugle, but rather identifies the bugle as a certain kind of instrument which has much in common with other instruments in the same class. Another instrument classified as 423.121.22 is the bronze [lur](/wiki/Lur "Lur"), an instrument dating back to the [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age").

## Suffixes and composite instruments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hornbostel%E2%80%93Sachs&action=edit&section=56 "Edit section: Suffixes and composite instruments")]

After the number described above, a number of [suffixes](/wiki/Suffix "Suffix") may be appended. An **8** indicates that the instrument has a keyboard attached, while a **9** indicates the instrument is mechanically driven. In addition to these, there are a number of suffixes unique to each of the top-level groups indicating details not considered crucial to the fundamental nature of the instrument. In the membranophone class, for instance, suffixes can indicate whether the skin of a drum is glued, nailed or tied to its body; in the chordophone class, suffixes can indicate whether the strings are plucked with fingers or plectrum, or played with a bow.

There are ways to classify instruments with this system even if they have elements from more than one group. Such instruments may have particularly long classification numbers with colons and hyphens used as well as numbers. Hornbostel and Sachs themselves cite the case of various [bagpipes](/wiki/Bagpipe "Bagpipe") where some of the pipes are single reed (like a clarinet) and others are double reed (like the [oboe](/wiki/Oboe "Oboe")). A number of similar composite instruments exist.
